title: Dog Scabs on Ear Margins: Causes, Urgency, and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn about scabs on dog ear margins. Understand why this is usually a low-urgency issue and how clear photos assist your veterinarian in diagnosis.
content_html:
TL;DR: Scabs on your dog's ear margins are usually non-emergency issues caused by irritants, parasites, or circulation problems, but they require a vet's evaluation to prevent worsening.

What causes scabs or crusting on the edges of my dog's ears?

Scabs or crusting along the edges of a dog's ears, known as ear margin dermatosis, can be caused by a variety of factors. These include environmental irritants like fly bites, inflammatory conditions like vasculitis, or even parasitic infections like sarcoptic mange. In some cases, it may be related to sebaceous gland issues or cold weather exposure that restricts blood flow to the thin skin of the ear tips.

Are scabs on my dog's ear tips considered a medical emergency?

  • The urgency level for scabs on ear margins is generally Low and rarely life-threatening.
  • While the condition can be itchy or lead to minor bleeding from head shaking, it is not an immediate crisis.
  • Schedule a routine appointment with your veterinarian to identify the cause and prevent infection or further cracking.

How can taking a photo of my dog's ears help with triage?

  • A high-quality, well-lit photograph allows a professional to see the specific distribution of scabs and hair loss.
  • Photos capture the color of the underlying skin for better diagnostic insight.
  • Comparing photos over a few days helps the vet determine if the condition is stable, improving, or spreading.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Ear margin scabs in dogs can be caused by ear margin seborrhea, sarcoptic mange, proliferative thrombovascular necrosis, dermatophytosis, or frostbite. Ear margin seborrhea commonly affects Dachshunds, although other breeds with pendulous pinnae may be affected. Lesions appear as waxy, gray to yellow scales adherent to the base of hair shafts, potentially progressing to edematous and fissured ear margins in severe cases. Dermatophytosis can cause scaling pinnal dermatitis, although the ear margin is not typically primarily involved. Differential diagnoses should be considered when evaluating ear margin lesions with associated scabs.

Chapter: Dermatology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 523)

faqs.question: Could my dog's ear scabs be caused by flies?
faqs.answer: Yes, 'fly strike' is a common cause where biting flies target the thin skin of the ear margins, leading to bloody crusts and scabbing, especially in outdoor dogs.

faqs.question: Are certain breeds more prone to ear margin issues?
faqs.answer: Yes, breeds with pendulous ears, such as Dachshunds, Cocker Spaniels, and Basset Hounds, are more frequently affected by ear margin dermatosis.

faqs.question: Should I try to peel the scabs off?
faqs.answer: No, you should avoid peeling the scabs as this can cause pain, bleeding, and increase the risk of a secondary bacterial infection. Let a vet evaluate the skin first.
